# How to Read Your Voice: The Two-Chart System

You have been speaking Mandinka your entire life. Your mouth already knows exactly what to do. These two charts do not teach you new sounds — they show you, for the first time, what your mouth is already doing when you speak. That is a different thing entirely.

When you write a Mandinka word, you are not guessing at letters. You are recording a physical event that happens inside your mouth. These charts are the map of that event.

## ![][image1]

## Chart 1: The Quick Reference Grid

**TONGUE POSITION (left to right) × MOUTH SHAPE (top to bottom)**

Chart 1 gives you two pieces of information at once.

**The columns (left to right)** tell you where your tongue is pointing or touching inside your mouth — from the very front near your teeth all the way back to where your tongue touches the soft part of the roof of your mouth near your throat.

**The rows (top to bottom)** tell you what shape your lips are making — from closed lips all the way to a wide open mouth.

Every sound in Mandinka lands somewhere on this grid. Find the row that matches your lip shape. Find the column that matches where your tongue is. The letter at that intersection is the sound you are making.

**The color** tells you the tongue zone. Red is in front. Orange moves back slightly. Yellow moves further back. Green is in the center of the mouth. Blue is in the back of the mouth. Purple is high back of the mouth. Black is the very back where your tongue touches the soft palate.

Use this chart as your first stop when you hear a sound and are not sure which letter it is. Ask yourself two questions:

1. What are my lips doing?  
2. Where is my tongue pointing?

The grid will give you the letter.

![][image2]

## Chart 2: Inside the Mouth

**THE ARTICULATION ARC**

Chart 2 shows you the same information from inside. The arc at the top represents the roof of your mouth — your palate — curving from your lips at the far left all the way back to your throat at the far right.

The dotted lines drop down from each sound category to the exact point on the tongue where that sound is made. The colored dot on the tongue arc shows you the contact point — where your tongue either touches or comes closest to the roof of your mouth to produce that sound.

Follow the color. If a sound is red in Chart 1, its dotted line and tongue dot are red in Chart 2\. The two charts use the same color system so you can move between them.

Chart 2 is especially useful for the sounds that are hardest to spell in Mandinka — the sounds that do not exist in English and that colonial spelling systems got wrong for a hundred years. The **ŋ** (ng) sound, for example, sits at the velar position — far back, where your tongue rises to touch the soft palate. The **ñ** (ny) sound sits at the palatal position, just in front of that. Once you see where those sounds live in your mouth, you will never confuse them again.

## Spelling a Complex Word: Step by Step

*\[WAITING FOR MUHAMMED — complex Mandinka word with difficult sounds to be inserted here. Word should include at minimum: a nasal consonant, a long vowel, and either ŋ or ñ. Walk through each sound one at a time using both charts.\]*

**Step 1 — Say the word slowly.** Break it into its individual sounds, not its syllables. Clap once for each distinct sound you hear.

**Step 2 — Take the first sound.** Ask: what are my lips doing? Find that row in Chart 1\. Now ask: where is my tongue? Find that column. The letter at the intersection is your first letter.

**Step 3 — Check Chart 2\.** Follow the dotted line for that letter down to the tongue arc. Place your tongue there and say the sound again. If it matches what you hear in the word, you have the right letter.

**Step 4 — Move to the next sound.** Repeat.

**Step 5 — Check vowel length.** For every vowel, hold it out loud. Is it short — one clap? Or long — two claps? If it is long, double the vowel in your spelling. This is not optional. In Mandinka, vowel length changes the meaning of the word entirely.

**Step 6 — Check tone.** Say the word in a full sentence. Does the meaning change if you raise or lower the pitch on any syllable? If yes, mark the tone. High tone gets (´). Low tone gets (\`).

**Step 7 — Write the complete word.** You have not guessed. You have recorded exactly what your mouth did.

This is how every word in this book was built. Not borrowed from English. Not approximated from French. Recorded from Mandinka mouths, using Mandinka sounds, mapped to a writing system built to hold them.

Your voice is the source. These charts are the tools. The word you spell is the record.
